comments: Archive sessions from the Rolling Stones

track_listing.track: Don't Ever Wanna Lose Ya (LP - New England) Infinity
Interview with Mick Jagger & Brian Jones of the Rolling Stones by Brian Matthew on Top Gear
The Gates Of Delirium (LP - Relayer) Atlantic
Black Maria (2xLP - Back To The Bars) Bearsville
(I Can't Get No) Satisfaction (Saturday Club session)
Oh Baby (We Got A Good Thing Goin') (Saturday Club session)

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Hi there. Good evening from a hot, humid and sticky London. This is Tommy Vance. Welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, in response to a heavy mailbag, our archive sessions are by the Rolling Stones.
We've got nine numbers by Jagger, Richards, Watts, Wyman and Jones. The original line-up. All recorded during the mid-sixties.
Here are classic cuts from Van Gelis, Robert Wyatt, yes, Todd Rundgren, and a 23-minute real classic by the Floyd.
We've got new releases from New England, Molly Hatchet and the Tubes, and of course the Friday Night Connection,
which this week is going to put a lot of people into a surprise state.
And tonight we start with Motorhead.
That's the Scorpions and the album version of the thing that's just come out as a single called Love Drive.
And of course it's also on the album Love Drive as well.
But of course the single is a little bit shorter.
Prior to that, you heard Overkill by Motorhead,
and we played it for a load of people,
including Beamish and Marcus and Anderson,
with lots of best wishes for Mike Armstrong
of Llandudno Junction in North Wales,
and also for all the mates of Eddie Mander
of I think it's Rush Dean or Rush Heen in Northampton.
Alistair McIntosh wanted me to play it
for Paul and Don and Russ at Shrewsbury School,
and everybody else who likes Motorhead.
And also, we had a letter here that says,
Punk is dead, heavy metal rules, disco will die.
Play Motorhead, or else signed Anonymous of South Glamorgan, so that should keep everybody happy.
But we don't particularly like to get anonymous letters. It's nice to know your names.
Here's something that you'll instantly recognize by Van Gelis.
That's one of the favorite tracks of the moment of Mr. Wilson, who's sitting right behind me.
From the LP New England by New England, the track is entitled Don't Ever Wanna Lose Ya.
Pride of the Latchy Heard, Pole Star by Van Gelis from the 1976 album Albedo 039.
and we played it for Mr. Edley
Edbanger Ord, aged all of 15
of 428 Kingswood Road
Grove Farm Estate in Nunez
who loves it
also, we were asked to play it by Derek
Wiggins for all his mates
Derek lives in number 9 Stratton Close
Bexley Heath in Kent
and as a PS on this yellow card
a somewhat ghastly yellow card that Derek sent in
he says, PS, be
cooperative
well spotted Derek
Friday Rock Show 10
23 and 40 seconds.
And now let's turn to our archive session
tonight, the first of three.
This was recorded on the 1st of the 3rd, 1965.
It features the original line-up
of the Rolling Stones. The songs you're going to hear
are Everybody Needs Somebody to Love,
The Last Time, and Down the Road a Piece.
But just before that,
here's a rather poignant interview
with Brian Matthew.
It's Top of the Pup's talking time with the Rolling Stones,
so meet Brian Jones
and Mick Jagger.
Now, Mick, you've achieved so very much on the international scene.
What is there left to make you want to go on?
What drives you on?
If you've ever got to do anything that you haven't done before.
Or if you look forward to something like going to a new country.
Or perhaps making a film or making new records are always something new.
Yes, Brian, do you visualise, in fact, the group going on for a long time?
Or do you think you're going to get to the stage where you think,
well, we've made it, let's get out of it?
Well, it's very difficult to say,
but certainly things seem to be as big now as they were.
I mean, there's no lessening of interest.
Reaction's sort of everywhere's good.
So at the moment, I'm not worried.
No.
But I can't sort of, you know, give any sort of long-term...
No, what I was getting at from your own point of view,
you'd like to go on for a long, long time.
I think so, yeah.
As long as, you know, things keep on happening,
as Mick says, as long as there's always something new to look forward to.
Well, there's something here that all your fans
have been looking forward to during the show, I'm sure.
You know, the boys were there first number today.
Everybody needs somebody to love.
Back in March of 1965,
they had the Rolling Stones and Down the Road
A Piece, The Last Time, and Everybody Needs
Somebody to Love.
And that's three out of nine
numbers that we've got by the mid-60s
Stones tonight.
If you ever feel the urge to write to us about
any requests or any old
archive sessions that you'd like us to dig out
of our library, just write to me.
I'm Tommy Vance, or you can write to the producer, Tony Wilson,
or both of us at the Friday Rock Show,
BBC Radio 1,
London W1A4WW,
and we'll go digging.
I'd just like to say here to a gentleman who very kindly sent in a little package to us from Red Car in Cleveland
and also wanted us to find young Peeley singing along with Bill Oddie on a thing called Harry Krishna.
If you'd like to get in contact with me personally, I'd be very, very grateful.
We don't have your address or your name.
If you'd like to contact me personally, well, you could be of great help.
It's 25 minutes now before 11 o'clock, and we thought, as it's a hot and sticky time in our country,
that something soft and interesting might go down well here.
Music there by Yes.
From the album Relayer, that's the end section of Gates of Delirium,
and it's called Soon.
And we played it for a gentleman by the name of Ian,
who's recently returned from Norwich.
I say returned because he's gone back to Ventnor in the Isle of Wight.
And we were asked to play it by Jan Anderson, not John Anderson,
who lives at Greenfields, down lane in Ventnor, on that beautiful Isle of Wight.
Before that, Robert Wyatt's Sea Song,
a little-known track that I don't think I've ever heard on the radio before.
And that was asked for by Luke McKiernan of No. 40 Tankerton Road, Whitstable in Kent,
who also says we don't play enough Caravan on the programme.
Well, I'll put that to rights on September 7th,
because September 7th, here on the Friday Rock Show,
the archive session will be by Caravan.
now it's 12 minutes to 11 let's go down to gator country that was todd rundgren and black mariah
from the double live lp back to the bar especially for a man who rates todd rundgren as his number one
that's roy retton of number five lansbury drive south normanton in derbyshire and before that you
heard the band by the name of molly hatchett who come from florida they've got an album an album
rather out of the moment on epic it's called molly hatchett but the track that we played you
called Gator Country, is on a sort of demonstration album
that was shunted around the radio stations
purely for demonstration purposes, we think.
We don't believe it's available commercially,
but they're a good, solid Florida band.
It's a minute after 11 o'clock.
People wrote in last week and said,
excuse me, we think you got it wrong,
and you were right, we did have it wrong.
The Rainbow album is titled Down to Earth, Not Rush.
I explain why we got it wrong on Rock On last week,
so let's not waste time again.
It is called Down to Earth.
Next week sees a big event, the first of two at Nebworth.
Now, as we mentioned last week,
if you'd like to get in on something we like to call the Nebworth Serenade,
and that's music to sort of soothe your nerves
as you're lying under the stars in tents,
although if this weather holds for next weekend, and I hope it does,
a tent you won't need, just a sleeping bag.
The type of music that you'd like to hear
if you were lying around the greenfields around Nebworth
waiting for Zeppelin and the rest to appear,
well, jot it down on a postcard and send it to Nebworth Serenade.
Sounds like a Radio 4 programme.
At the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
You don't have to be going to do it.
Just the type of music you'd like to hear next Friday.
Back to 65 and more from the Stones.
Make way now for the stars of our show, the Rolling Stones.
The Rolling Stones, and I can't get no satisfaction
before that cry to me and prior to that, mercy, mercy.
Recorded in 1965 in the BBC studios, I'll never forget.
Introducing the Rolling Stones in Los Angeles
At a consulate, concert rather
Not a consulate
In about 1964
They started with that number
And for about an hour and fifty minutes
You couldn't hear a thing
The crowd went berserk
Okay, it's time for the Friday Night Connection
Bit of a surprise here
Last week we played you
Nino Frisco Blues by Eric Clapton
You By My Side by Chris Quire
And Shape of Things by Roger Chapman
Of all the letters that came in up till 5 o'clock this afternoon here to the BBC,
there was one, I say again, one right entry.
And it comes from Billy Hartley of No. 25 Seaview Walk, Merton, Seaham, County, Durham.
Bill, you must be smiling at the moment.
You are the only person to get it right.
Bill Rowe, dear Tom, the Friday night connection for the 23rd of July
was that the album titles included the nicknames of the artists featured,
Namely, album title Chapo, artist Roger Chapman.
He's called Chapo.
Fish Out of Water was the name of the album,
from which You By My Side came.
Chris Squire, the artist, he's nicknamed The Fish.
And the album for Mr. Clapton was Slow Hand,
and everybody knows that one.
William, you get yourself a £10 record token,
a large, sizable round of applause from myself and Mr. Wilson,
neither of whom I would think would have actually have got it
had it been put up to us,
And this is Mr Hartley's own Friday Night Connection.
1A4WW.
Do it as fast as you can.
Don't forget, you could win yourself a £10 record token.
Now, seeing that Billy Hartley
got the only correct answer this week,
it must be rather like winning the pools, Bill.
Unfortunately, we haven't got half a million quid to give you,
but the round of applause is very genuine,
and also the £10 record token is coming your way.
Don't forget to send your cards and answers
to the Friday Night Connection,
Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1,
London W1A4WW.
Back to the Stones.
Rolling Stones, as they were, on the 1st of the 10th in 1965,
the original line-up on our last set by then tonight,
Fanny Mae, Spider and the Fly, and baby, we got a good thing going.
Talking of good things, I've got a nice letter here.
It goes like this.
Dear Tom, I'm a Pink Floyd fan. I'm only 15.
I have friends who are disco freaks.
I'm constantly having the wee-wee taken out of me for liking the Floyd.
I am depressed.
My girlfriend left me because she thought listening to Pink Floyd has made me dull.
I couldn't afford a ticket to see the Floyd in London, so help.
A few years ago, in September 1971 or 72,
the Floyd played for John Peel's Top Gear
a piece called Return of the Son of Nothing.
This name was changed to Echoes for the show.
It is my favourite piece of music.
If you want to cheer me up a bit, play it.
I'm sure you'll make a few other people happy too.
Yours hopefully, a Floyd fan from Bristol, Tim.
It's a hazard that the Floyd have finished their new album,
but nobody knows when it's coming out.
That was Echoes from the 1971 Pink Floyd album Metal.
Music for a summer's night.
Next week on the Friday Rock Show,
Wishbone Ash, our archive session.
And don't forget the Nebworth Serenade,
music to be sitting under the stars
by waiting for the emergence of Led Zeppelin.
Forget not to Mike Reed and the Pee-Lo,
weeknights on Radio 1.
Tomorrow, Rock On is going to come live from Manchester
and it's going to reflect what's happening up
in that rather exciting part of our country.
Tonight, our engineer was Chris.
our producer as always on the friday rock show is tony wilson roy says hello to his mum i'll talk
to you from manchester tomorrow wherever you are take care have a good time it's midnight and we're
together on all wavelengths 433 330 285 275 and vhf this is liz allen in the midnight newsroom
the headlines the queen is in zambia and for the first time a political note has crept in
The Vatican, say a visit by the Pope to Northern Ireland, has never been contemplated
Derbyshire villagers are evacuated because of the danger of a gas explosion
The Queen has arrived in the Zambian capital, Lusaka, on the final leg of her African tour
She'll be attending the opening of the Commonwealth Conference
despite fears for her safety because of the continuing guerrilla war in neighbouring Zimbabwe, Rhodesia
For the first time on the tour, there was a direct political note in the crowd
which welcomed the Queen in the form of banners and placards
bearing strong messages for the Royal Party.
From Lusaka, John Osman reports.
One slogan read,
Queen of the UK should not remain impotent over Zimbabwe.
No sell-out in your address to the Commonwealth Conf.
The slogan was clearly a reference to the stand on Rhodesia taken by Mrs Thatcher,
a policy over which many African and Commonwealth states
have expressed doubts, but the propagandist who manufactured the slogan
is clearly unaware that normally the Queen is above politics
and does not, in fact, address the conference itself.
Nevertheless, he's made his point to a major audience.
Kenya is reported to have expelled several thousand Ugandans
after large-scale police round-ups in the Kenyan capital Nairobi and other areas.
More expulsions are expected to follow.
The move comes after a series of crimes, including murder and robbery,
which have been blamed on Ugandans living in Kenya.
The Vatican has told Britain that the Pope has never contemplated
visiting Northern Ireland during his stay in the Irish Republic at the end of September.
This categorical statement has surprised Irish bishops.
They had recommended that the Pope should visit Armagh, Londonderry and Belfast,
as well as a number of places in the Republic.
The Government have announced a new scheme to help council house tenants
to get part ownership of their own homes.
At a conference in Nottingham, the Housing Minister, John Stanley,
gave details of what he called a dramatic breakthrough
for tenants who can't afford to buy their council property outright.
Here's Peter Ruff.
Government policy remains for as many tenants to buy in one go as possible,
but this scheme is for those who can't.
A halfway house was the Minister's way of describing it this evening.
The details have still to be worked out and will be published in the new housing bill later in this session of Parliament.
But what they have in mind is for tenants to take a share in their household flat.
In effect, their rent each week will become a repayment on a local authority loan.
The Government claim that surveys tell them that there's a growing demand for ownership by tenants.
But there is bound to be acrimonious debate, inside and outside Parliament, about the new plan.
Local authorities often warn that they'll be left to administer properties
which are already difficult to rent, let alone sell.
But the Minister is adamant that the opportunities must be given,
while accepting that more study must be made of bad council accommodation.
Negotiators in the dispute at Times newspapers
have agreed on a formula which could lead to publication being resumed.
The agreement followed eight hours of talks
between leaders of the main unions involved
and the directors of the Thompson organisation and Times newspapers.
Details have not been disclosed. It now has to be put to union members.
ITV programmes on London Weekend television were blacked out this evening.
The company said they had gone off the air because of action by production staff involved in a pay dispute.
London Weekend hope to be screening programmes again in the morning.
About 1,500 people have been moved out of the village of Hayfield in Derbyshire because of fears of a gas explosion.
The emergency began after a council vehicle carrying liquid propane gas crashed into a wall.
This report from our Northern Affairs correspondent, Ralph Smith.
Villagers were at first reluctant to obey police loud hailer warnings to move out of the area,
but when the message sunk in, they soon left in cars and coaches and ambulances for the old and disabled.
Even a budgerigar in a cage was taken to the safety of a school hall in a neighbouring village.
It's more than six hours now since the leak was discovered
in one of two 300-gallon tanks on the overturned tar-burning machine.
The spilt liquid is being washed away by firemen
who are also advising on the delicate task of righting the vehicle.
A local council spokesman said there was no danger of an explosion at the moment
and the evacuation was just a precaution.
The police still hope that those evacuated
will be able to sleep tonight in their own beds.
After six years of marriage,
the entertainer Bruce Forsyth and his wife Anthea have decided to separate.
They married while working together on BBC Television's The Generation Game.
They have two children, one of them adopted.
Their solicitors say the Forsyths are parting on friendly terms.
Finally, the weather.
England and Wales will be hot with sunny periods,
especially in the south and east.
Some thundery showers are likely later.
Scotland and Northern Ireland will be cooler with some rain.
Well, that's all from the newsroom for now.
the next news is at 1am.
